6.question: In whose hands the income of Piya shall be taxable? Also, compute the amount of income that shall be taxable if both parents pay tax under default tax regime.
6.student_answer: Income of ₹ 20,11,500 shall be taxable in the hands of Piyus father or mother whose Income before clubbing is higher.
6.model_answer:
Answer.(d)

Income of ₹ 20,13,000 shall be taxable in the hands of Piya’s father or mother, whose income before this clubbing is higher.

7.question: Rohit, a resident Indian, has incurred ₹ 15,000 for medical treatment of his dependent brother, who is a person with severe disability and has deposited ₹ 20,000 with LIC for his maintenance. Rohit shifts out of the default tax regime for A.Y. 2024-25. Rohit would be eligible for deduction under section 80DD of an amount equal to –
7.model_answer: 4. Rohit, a resident Indian, has incurred ₹ 15,000 for medical treatment of his dependent brother, who is a person with severe disability and has deposited ₹ 20,000 with LIC for his maintenance. Rohit shifts out of the default tax regime for A.Y. 2024-25. Rohit would be eligible for deduction under section 80DD of an amount equal to – (a) ₹ 15,000 (b) ₹ 35,000 (c) ₹ 75,000 (d) ₹ 1,25,000 (1 Mark) Answer.(d)

8.question: What is the residential status of Mr. Sushil for A.Y. 2024-25 and his income liable to tax in India during A.Y. 2024-25?
8.student_answer: Resident but not ordinarily Resident ₹ 11,75,000 is liable to tax in India.
8.model_answer: Answer.(a) Non-Resident; ₹ 6,25,000 is liable to tax in India

a.question:
Mr. Kunal (age 27 years) is an employee in a private company posted in Delhi. He was appointed on 01.02.2023 in the scale of ₹ 60,000 - ₹ 1,000 - ₹ 70,000. He furnishes you the following information for the previous year 2024-25:
(i) Dearness allowance @25% of basic salary (60% of DA forms part of retirement benefits)
(ii) Bonus equal to one month salary. Paid in November 2024 on basic salary applicable for that month.
(iii) Leave encashment for P.Y. 2024-25 of ₹ 10,000.
(iv) He also received a motor car on 01.12.2024 (cubic capacity of engine exceeds 1.60 litres) along with chauffeur for both official and personal purpose. The motor car is owned by his employer and all expenses are met by the employer.
(v) His employer granted him a loan of ₹ 2,00,000 on 1st June, 2024 which is repayable in equal quarterly installments over 2 years starting from 1st October, 2024. The State Bank of India (SBI) lending rate for such loans is 9.5% per annum as on 01.04.2024, while the employer recovers interest @5.5% per annum from the employee.
(vi) His employer gave him a rent-free accommodation (fully furnished) in Delhi from 01.04.2023. This house is owned by the employer. The perquisite value of such furnished rent-free accommodation during the previous year 2023-24 was valued at ₹ 92,000. Further, the accommodation is continued to be provided by the employer to the employee in P.Y. 2024-25 also.
(vii) The furniture and appliances provided with the house were bought by the employer at an aggregate cost of ₹ 1,50,000 on 01.01.2023. Electricity and water bills of ₹ 5,000 p.m. for the said house were paid by the employer.
(viii) His colleagues gifted him a mobile phone worth ₹ 45,000 from their own contribution on account of his marriage.
You are required to compute the income chargeable under the head Salaries in the hands of Mr. Kunal for the Assessment Year 2025-26 assuming that he wants to pay tax under default tax regime under section 115BAC.
a.student_answer:
Computation of Total Income under the Head Salary in the hands of Mr. Kunal for the Assessment Year 2025-26 (115BAC)

| Particulars | ₹ | ₹ |
|-------------|---|---|
| 1) Basic Salary | | 7,22,000 |
| (60,000x10) + (61,000x2) | | |
| 2) Dearness Allowance | | 1,80,500 |
| 3) Bonus | | 60,000 |
| 4) Leave Encashment | | |
| (Note 1) | | |
| 5) Loan given at | | 2,00,000 |
| Lower Rate (Note 2) | | |
| 6) Rent free | | 92,000 |
| Accommodation | | |
| Accommodation (Note 3) | | |
| 7) Furniture provided | | 15,000 |
| free of cost | | |
| (Note 4) | | |
| 8) Electricity & | | 60,000 |
| Water bill paid | | |
| by the employer | | |
| (5000 x 12) | | |
| 9) Gift from colleague | | Nil |
| (Note 5) | | |
| Total Salary | | 13,29,500 |
| chargeable to tax | | |

Notes:
1) Since Assessee is opting for 115BAC entire Leave encashment become taxable.
2) Since it has opten for 115BAC entire loan become taxable.
3) In 115BAC entire Rent Free Accomodation provided by the employer are taxable.
4) The value of furniture & appliances shall be taken at 10% of Actual Cost Less Recovered which is (150,000 x 10%) = 15000.
5) Gift of mobile phone does not cover in the definition of Property And as per 56(2)(x) it is not chargeable to tax.
a.feedback: The student has covered most components and performed basic calculations, but there are several conceptual and calculation errors. The basic salary and DA calculations are incorrect, and the perquisite value of rent-free accommodation should be recomputed for the current year, not just carried forward. The loan perquisite is not the loan amount but the interest differential. The bonus and leave encashment treatment is correct. The treatment of the gift is correct. More detailed working and correct perquisite valuation are needed for full marks.
key_points_covered: Included basic salary, DA, bonus, leave encashment
Included rent-free accommodation and furniture perquisite
Included electricity and water bills paid by employer
Correctly treated gift from colleagues as not taxable
key_points_missed: Incorrect calculation of basic salary and DA
Incorrect perquisite value for loan (should be interest differential, not loan amount)
Rent-free accommodation perquisite should be recomputed for current year
No calculation for car perquisite included
No breakup of DA for retirement benefits
No detailed calculation for leave encashment exemption (if any)

a.question:
Mr. Akshay, a resident individual, provides the following details of his income/losses for the year ended 31.03.2025:
(i) Income from salary (computed) 35,20,000
(ii) Rent received from house property situated in Delhi 5,00,000
(iii) Repayment of loan taken for purchase of above property. Loan was taken from a friend. This repayment includes payment of interest of ₹ 7,00,000
(iv) Rent received from house property situated in Jaipur 3,20,000
(v) Interest on loan taken for repair of house properties situated in Mumbai and Delhi. The property in Mumbai is self-occupied. Loan was taken on 01.04.17 and was utilized in 50:50 ratio for house properties situated in Mumbai and Delhi, respectively.
(vi) Long-term capital gains on sale of equity shares on which STT has been paid 7,95,000
(vii) Interest on fixed deposit 73,000
(viii) Loss from textile business 7,50,000
(ix) Speculation business profit 2,30,000
(x) Lottery income (Gross) 75,000
(xi) Loss incurred by the firm in which he is a partner 1,60,000
(xii) Salary received as a partner from partnership firm. The same was allowed to firm 50,000
(xiii) Life insurance premium paid for his son who is 30 years of age and is working in USA 15,000
He has the following brought forward losses of A.Y. 2024-25
Brought forward short-term capital loss on sale of gold 2,75,000
Brought forward loss on sale of equity shares u/s 111A 25,000
Compute total income of Mr. Akshay for the assessment year 2025-26 and the amount of loss that can be carried forward. Mr. Akshay has opted out of the default tax regime.

Computation of Total Income of Mr. Akshay  for the A.Y Assessment year 25-26

|-------------|---|---|
| 1) Income from Salary | 35,20,000 | |
| (-) House Property loss | (200,000) | 33,20,000 |
| 2) Rent received from | | |
| property situated in Delhi | 500,000 | |
| (-) Standard Deduction | 150,000 | |
| U/s 24(a) 30% | | |
| Net Annual Value | 350,000 | |
| (-) Interest on Borrowed | 700,000 | |
| Capital U/s 24(b) | 75,000 | |
| Loss from House | (350,000) | |
| Property in Delhi | (425,000) | |
| 3) Rent from property | | |
| situated in Jaipur | 320,000 | |
| (-) Standard Deduction | 96,000 | |
| U/s 24(a) | | |
| Net Annual Value | 224,000 | |
| 4) Property situated in | | |
| Mumbai | | |
| GAV Gross Annual Value | Nil | |
| (-) Interest on borrowed | (75,000) | |

| Loss from self-occupied | (30,000) | |
| Property | (75,000) | |

Total Loss from House Property (231,000)

1) Long-term Capital Gain 795,000
(-) Loss of textile business (397,000)
(-) Brought forward Loss (295,275,000)
on sale of gold

from his friend no deduction
under 80C will be available to

a.feedback: The student attempted to compute total income and considered most heads of income, but there are several conceptual and calculation errors. The house property computation is unclear and contains mistakes in interest deduction and set-off. The capital gains section is incorrect, especially in the treatment of brought forward losses and set-off rules. Business income and other sources are not properly segregated, and the set-off of losses is not as per the Income Tax Act. The deduction under section 80C is not available for life insurance premium paid for a major son who is not dependent and is a non-resident. The answer lacks clarity and proper structure.
key_points_covered: Attempted computation under all heads of income
Considered set-off and carry forward of losses
Mentioned restriction on house property loss set-off
Identified that loan from friend is not eligible for 80C
key_points_missed: Correct calculation and set-off of house property losses (interest deduction limits, self-occupied property interest cap)
Proper treatment and set-off of brought forward capital losses (short-term and long-term)
Correct computation of business income (speculation vs. non-speculation, partnership firm loss treatment)
Accurate calculation of gross total income and total income
Correct application of section 80C deduction rules (life insurance premium for non-dependent, non-resident son not eligible)
Proper segregation and calculation of income from other sources

i.question:
Examine the applicability of tax deduction at source provisions, the rate and amount of tax deduction in the following cases for the F.Y. 2024-25:
(i) Payment made by a company to Mr. Ram, sub-contractor, ₹3,00,000 with outstanding balance of ₹1,20,000 shown in the books as on 31.3.2025.
i.student_answer: As per section 194C if the contract value exceed ₹ 100,000 in aggregate and ₹ 30,000 in single payment then TDS shall be deducted at 1%, for Individual and Hindu-undivided family and 2% for others. The tax deduction at source in this case will be = 300,000 x 1% = 3000
i.feedback: The student correctly identified the relevant section (194C), threshold limits, and TDS rate for an individual sub-contractor. The calculation of TDS is also correct. However, the answer does not discuss the timing of deduction (i.e., TDS to be deducted at the time of credit or payment, whichever is earlier), nor does it address whether TDS is to be deducted on the outstanding balance. The answer could be improved by mentioning these aspects.
key_points_covered: Section 194C applicability
Threshold limits for TDS
Correct TDS rate for individual sub-contractor
Correct calculation of TDS amount
key_points_missed: Timing of TDS deduction (credit or payment, whichever is earlier)
Clarification on TDS applicability on outstanding balance

ii.question:
Examine the applicability of tax deduction at source provisions, the rate and amount of tax deduction in the following cases for the F.Y. 2024-25:
(ii) Winning from horse race ₹1,50,000 paid to Mr. Shyam, an Indian resident.
ii.student_answer: As per Section 194BB if Winning from & Horse Races exceed ₹ 10,000 in a year then TDS @ at the rate 30% will be applicable. Therefore tax deducted in this case will be = 150,000 x 30% = 45,000
ii.feedback: Excellent answer. The student correctly identified the relevant section (194BB), the threshold limit (₹10,000), the applicable TDS rate (30%), and accurately calculated the amount of tax to be deducted (₹45,000).
key_points_covered: Correct section (194BB)
Threshold limit of ₹10,000
Applicable TDS rate of 30%
Correct calculation of TDS amount

iii.question:
Examine the applicability of tax deduction at source provisions, the rate and amount of tax deduction in the following cases for the F.Y. 2024-25:
(iii) ₹2,00,000 paid to Mr. A, a resident individual, on 22-02-2025 by the State of Uttar Pradesh on compulsory acquisition of his urban land.
iii.student_answer: As per Section 194LA in case of compulsary compulsory acquisition if the compensation exceed ₹ 250,000 in a year then only TDS provision will be applicable. Since the consideration does not exceed ₹ 200,000 then there will be no tax deducted at such source.
iii.feedback: The student correctly identified the relevant section (194LA) and the threshold limit of ₹2,50,000 for TDS applicability. However, the answer contains minor errors in wording and does not mention the TDS rate or clarify that the payment is for urban land, which is covered under the section. The answer also does not explicitly state that the payer is the State Government, which is relevant for completeness.
key_points_covered: Section 194LA applies
Threshold of ₹2,50,000 for TDS applicability
No TDS since payment is ₹2,00,000
key_points_missed: TDS rate (10%) if applicable
Explicit mention that urban land is covered
Payer is State Government

a.question:
Mr. Yogesh constructed a house in P.Y. 2017-18 with 3 independent units. During the P.Y. 2023-24, Unit - 1 (50% of floor area) is let out for residential purpose at monthly rent of ₹ 20,000. Rent of January, 2024 could not be collected from the tenant and a notice to vacate the unit was given to the tenant. No other property of Mr. Yogesh is occupied by the tenant. Unit - 1 remains vacant for February and March 2024 when it is not put to any use. Unit - 2 (25% of the floor area) is used by Mr. Yogesh for the purpose of his business, while Unit - 3 (the remaining 25%) is utilized for the purpose of his residence. Other particulars of the house are as follows:
Municipal valuation - ₹ 2,88,000
Fair rent - ₹ 2,98,000
Standard rent under the Rent Control Act - ₹ 2,78,000
Municipal taxes - ₹ 30,000 paid by Mr. Yogesh Repairs - ₹ 7,000
Interest on capital borrowed for the construction of the property - ₹ 90,000,
Ground rent - ₹ 6,000 and
Fire insurance premium paid - ₹ 60,000.
Income of Yogesh from the business is ₹ 2,40,000 (without debiting house rent and other incidental expenditure).
Determine the taxable income of Mr. Yogesh for the assessment year 2024-25 if he pays tax under section 115BAC.
key_points_missed: Answer not attempted

b.question:
Mr. Soham, a builder, entered into an agreement on 1.4.2023 with Mr. Aman to transfer 4th Floor in Tower A of a new project for ₹ 1,50,00,000. He received ₹ 25 lakhs as advance in cash on 1.4.2023. The stamp duty value of such floor on that date was ₹ 1,70,00,000. The sale deed was executed and registered on 15.6.2023 for the agreed consideration. However, the stamp duty value on that date was ₹ 1,75,00,000.
Discuss the tax consequences of above, in the hands of Mr. Soham and Mr. Aman.
key_points_missed: Answer not attempted

a.question:
Vijay Prasad, a non resident aged 50 years furnishes the following information of the income from India for the year ended on 31-03-2024:
Income by way of salary (computed) 2,75,000
Short term capital loss (1,85,000)
Business income - Retail business 1,20,000
Business income - whole sale business (1,00,000)
Brought forward business loss (A.Y. 2021-22) (1,35,000)
Long term capital gain from sale of building 2,00,000
Lottery winnings (gross) 45,000
Contribution to provident fund and NSC 1,50,000
Income of minor daughter Manisha from special talent 2,00,000
Compute his income tax liability assuming that he opts out of the default tax regime under section 115BAC.
a.student_answer:
Computation of Total Income Tax Liability of Mr. Vijay Prasad for Assessment year 2025-26.

| Particulars | ₹ | ₹ |
|-------------|---|---|
| 1) Income from salary | | 275,000 |
| 2) Income from business | 120,000 | |
| Retail | | |
| (-) Brought forward loss | (100,000) | - Nil |
| (-) Brought forward loss | 20,000 | |
| 3) Capital Gain | | |
| a) Long-term Capital Gain | 200,000 | |
| (-) Short Term Capital Loss | (185,000) | |
| (-) Business Loss brought forward | (15,000) | → Nil |
| 4) Income from other sources | | |
| a) Lottery winnings | 45,000 | 45,000 |
| 5) Income of minor daughter | Nil | |
| (Since as per the Sec 64(1A) provision if minor daughter has earned Income out of talent it should not be clubbed) | | |

Gross Total Income 320,000
(-) Deduction under
Chapter VIA
80C (Contribution to
provident fund & NSC) (150,000)
__________
Total Income 170,000
(Since his total
Income does not exceeds
Basic exemption limit that
is ₹ 250,000 there
will be no tax liability.)
a.feedback: The student demonstrated a good understanding of the computation steps, including clubbing provisions for minor's income, set-off and carry forward of losses, and deductions under Chapter VIA. However, there are calculation errors and misapplication of set-off rules: (1) Short-term capital loss can only be set off against capital gains, not business income; (2) Brought forward business loss should be set off only against business income, not capital gains; (3) The gross total income and taxable income are incorrectly computed. The student correctly excluded the minor's income and applied the basic exemption limit, but did not compute tax on lottery winnings at the special rate. Deduction under 80C is correctly considered.
key_points_covered: Clubbing provisions for minor's income from special talent not applicable
Deduction under section 80C considered
Consideration of basic exemption limit
key_points_missed: Correct set-off of short-term capital loss only against capital gains
Correct set-off of brought forward business loss only against business income
Special rate of tax on lottery winnings not applied
Incorrect computation of gross total income and taxable income

b.question:
Mr. Kailash, a resident and ordinarily resident in India, could not file his return of Income for the assessment year 2021-22 before due date prescribed under section 139(1). Advise Mr. Kailash as a tax consultant.
What are the consequences for non-filing of return of Income within the due date under section 139(1)?
b.student_answer:
If Return of Income under section 139(1) the following will be the consequence;
1) Loss from Business & Profession cannot be carried forward
2) Capital Gain Loss cannot be carried forward
3) Fee under section 234F will be applicable that is ₹ 5000 or 1000 if total Income does not exceed ₹ 500,000
4) Interest will be applicable as per the provisions of the Act.
b.feedback: The student has correctly identified most key consequences: loss of carry forward of business and capital losses, fee under section 234F with correct amounts, and applicability of interest. However, the answer could be improved by specifying the type of interest (u/s 234A), mentioning that belated return can still be filed, and that certain deductions/exemptions may be disallowed. Overall, good conceptual understanding.
key_points_covered: Loss from Business & Profession cannot be carried forward
Capital Gain Loss cannot be carried forward
Fee under section 234F with correct amounts
Interest will be applicable
key_points_missed: Type of interest (u/s 234A)
Possibility to file belated return
Certain deductions/exemptions may be disallowed

3. Applied for issue of credit card to SBI

2.question: Which of the following statements is correct in relation to the re-availament of the input tax credit that had been reversed earlier, upon payment of disputed amount by the company to Rudraksh Enterprises?
2.student_answer: ITC can be reavailed without any limits after making payment of disputed amount along with tax payable thereon to Rudraksh Enterpricer.

Determine the validity of E-Challan generated by Vedika & Co. for payment of taxes for the month of November?

7.question: Vikas Nigam Limited (a Public Sector Undertaking) has placed an order to Bharti Steels registered in Bokaro, Orissa for supply of 70 Iron shields each costing ₹ 12000 (exclusive of GST). However, the supply will take place in 3 lots containing 10 shields, 20 shields, 40 shields on different days. Determine whether tax is required to be deducted under GST law by Vikas Nigam Limited on the above order?
7.student_answer: TDS will be deducted on the entire order of 70 solid shields.

8.question: ABC Insurance Ltd. received a proposal for pandemic insurance for cricket tournament organised by Lion’s Club. Sum assured for said insurance was ₹ 20 Crores with a premium of ₹ 5 lakh. The company issued the said policy on 1st July. The invoice for the same was issued on 5th August. Premium was received on 14th August. Determine the time of supply of service provided to Lion’s Club?

a.question:
Surya is engaged in providing a bouquet of goods and services. It is registered in Jaipur, Rajasthan. It provides the following information for the month of January:
(i) Organised a business exhibition in Gujarat for Jignesh Industries, registered in Surat, Gujarat
(ii) Provided accommodation services to 10 CA students (originally from outside Rajasthan) in a hostel – Surya Homes, owned by it. Surya Homes is located in Jaipur, Rajasthan. [Hostel accommodation charges are ₹ 22,000 per student per month. As per agreement, minimum period of stay of the students in hostel is 4 months.]
(iii) Performed the carnatic music to promote a brand of readymade garments – Rigley Garments, registered in Udaipur, Rajasthan.
(iv) Intra-State services provided as a business correspondent of Manimani Bank, registered in Alwar, Rajasthan, with respect to accounts in its Jaipur city branch.
(v) Rented a commercial property in Jaipur, Rajasthan to Ganga Ltd., a supplier of goods and services registered in Jodhpur, Rajasthan. Surya is a director in Ganga Ltd.
(vi) Sponsored a Business Summit organized in Bikaner, Rajasthan, by Associated Chamber of Commerce and paid a sponsorship fee of ₹ 5,00,000 to Associated Chamber of Commerce, registered in Jaipur, Rajasthan.
(vii) Received the services of transportation of goods by road from Sindhu Transporters, an unregistered Goods Transport Agency of Jodhpur, Rajasthan.
(viii) Taken cars on rental basis from Ajay Limited, registered in Jodhpur, Rajasthan.
Notes:
(i) Rates of CGST, SGST and IGST are 9%, 9% and 18% respectively for both inward and outward supply of goods and services except the car rental service and service of transportation of goods by GTA, on which the rates of CGST, SGST and IGST are 2.5%, 2.5% and 5% respectively.
(ii) All the amounts given above are exclusive of taxes, wherever applicable.
(iii) There was no opening balance of the ITC for the relevant period.
From the information given above, you are required to compute the minimum net GST liability payable in cash (CGST, SGST or IGST, as the case may be) for the month of January for Surya.
a.student_answer:
Computation of Net GST Payable in cash for the month of January of Surya

Particulars                Value      CGST     SGST     IGST

Tax payable on outward supplier-
a) Organised business   20,00,000                      360,000
   exhibition in Gujarat                               (20,00,000 x 18%)
   (since services are rendered in Gujarat Place of Supply is in Gujarat)

b) Provided accommodation 220,000   19,800   19,800
   service                (220,000 x 9%)
   (Since the room rent exceeds ₹ 20000 per month the said service is not exempt)

c) Intra-state           120,000   10,800   10,800
   service provided to Bank as a business correspondent (Since the services are supplied in urban area they are not exempt)

a) Performed carnatic music (Since the services are performed in the capacity of Brand Ambassador they are not exempt also)

e) Rented commercial property to Ganga Ltd in which Surya a director (Since he is a director & rented in the capacity of director no gst is payable on this)

c) Total output tax liability (-) ITC of SGST & CGST used to pay CGST liability & CGST liability Total GST Payable in cash under forward charge mechanism (+) RCM liability

Total GST Payable in cash

Computation of ITC available.

| Particular | Value | CGST | SGST | IGST |
|------------|-------|------|------|------|
| 1) Paid sponsor- | 500,000 | 45,000 | 45,000 | - |
| sponsorship fee | | | | |
| (Since sponsorship | | | | |
| services are provided | | | | |
| by a body corporate | | | | |
| then they are payable | | | | |
| under forward charge | | | | |
| mechanism so ITC | | | | |
| in respect of this | | | | |
| will be available.) | | | | |
| 2) Received transportation | 200,000 | 5,000 | 5,000 | |
| services from unregistered | | | | |
| Goods Transport agency | | | | |
| (Since it is not | | | | |
| registered tax will | | | | |
| be payable under | | | | |
| Reverse charge basis.) | | | | |
| 3) Taken car on | 150,000 | - | - | - |
| Rent | | | | |
| (ITC on cars is | | | | |
| blocked under section | | | | |
| 17(5)(a) | | | | |
| Total ITC Available | | 50,000 | 50,000 | - |
a.feedback: The student identified some correct GST treatments (e.g., exhibition as IGST, accommodation as taxable, business correspondent service as taxable, sponsorship under RCM, GTA under RCM, ITC on cars blocked). However, the answer is incomplete and contains calculation errors, missing values, and lacks a clear computation of net GST liability. Key steps like total output tax, RCM computation, and ITC set-off are not shown. Some explanations are correct, but the answer lacks structure and final figures.
key_points_covered: Correctly identified IGST for exhibition in Gujarat
Accommodation service taxable due to value exceeding exemption limit
Business correspondent service taxable
Sponsorship under RCM and ITC available
GTA service under RCM
Blocked ITC on car rental
key_points_missed: No calculation of total output tax liability
No calculation of RCM liability and its set-off
No clear computation of net GST payable in cash
Carnatic music service not quantified or taxed
Commercial property rent GST treatment incorrect (should be taxable, not exempt)
No stepwise set-off of ITC against output tax

1.question: Parth of Pune, Maharashtra enters into an agreement to sell goods to Bakul of Bareilly, Uttar Pradesh. While the goods were being packed in Pune godown of Parth, Bakul got an order from Shreyas of Shimoga, Karnataka for the said goods. Bakul agreed to supply the said goods to Shreyas and asked Parth to deliver the goods to Shreyas at Shimoga. You are required to determine the place of supply(ies) in the above situation.
1.student_answer: As per the provision of section 10 when goods are supplied in Bill to ship to Model the place of supply will be the principal place of business of buyer. Therefore in the given case since the goods are delivered delivered to third party i.e. Shreyar of Shimoga Karnataka on request of by Parth of Pune, Maharashtra on request of the Bokul of Bareilly, Uttar Pradesh. Therefore the place of supply will be Bareilly Uttar Pradesh.
1.feedback: The student correctly identified the 'Bill to Ship to' model and applied Section 10 of the IGST Act, stating that the place of supply is the principal place of business of the buyer (Bakul, Bareilly, UP). The answer is conceptually correct and covers the main point. However, the answer could be improved by explicitly mentioning that there are two supplies: (1) Parth to Bakul (place of supply: Bareilly, UP) and (2) Bakul to Shreyas (place of supply: Shimoga, Karnataka), as per Section 10(1)(b).
key_points_covered: Identified Bill to Ship to model
Correctly determined place of supply as Bareilly, UP for Parth to Bakul
key_points_missed: Did not mention the second supply from Bakul to Shreyas and its place of supply (Shimoga, Karnataka)
Did not cite Section 10(1)(b) specifically

2.question: Damani Industries has recruited Super Events Pvt. Ltd., an event management company of Gujarat, for organising the grand party for the launch of its new product at Bangalore. Damani Industries is registered in Mumbai. Determine the place of supply of the services provided by Super Events Pvt. Ltd. to Damani Industries. Will your answer be different if the product launch party is organised at Dubai?
2.student_answer: In case of supply of services of organising the event the place of supply if the recipient is registered his principal place of business where he is registered. And they when are supplied to unregistered persons place of supply will be where the event actually held as per section 12. Therefore in the given since Damini Industries is Registered in Mumbai, Place of Supply will be Mumbai. No, the answer will remain same if even if the product launch party is organised at @ Dubai.
2.feedback: The student correctly identified that for a registered recipient, the place of supply is the recipient's location (Mumbai). However, the answer incorrectly states that the place of supply would remain the same even if the event is held in Dubai. As per Section 12(7)(b) of the IGST Act, if the event is held outside India, the place of supply is the location of the recipient. The student missed referencing the relevant section for events held outside India and did not clearly distinguish between domestic and international events.
key_points_covered: Place of supply for registered recipient is the location of the recipient (Mumbai)
Reference to Section 12
key_points_missed: Correct explanation for events held outside India (Dubai)
Clear distinction between domestic and international event place of supply
Precise reference to Section 12(7) of IGST Act

b.question:
Dhun Pvt. Ltd. owned by Jairaj - a famous classical singer - wishes to organise a ‘Jairaj Music Concert’ in Gurugram (Haryana). Dhun Pvt. Ltd. (registered in Ludhiana, Punjab) enters into a contract with an event management company, Dhanraj (P) Ltd. (registered in Delhi) for organising the said music concert at an agreed consideration of Rs 10,00,000.
Dhanraj (P) Ltd. books the lawns of Hotel Dumdum, Gurugram (registered in Haryana) for holding the music concert, for a lump sum consideration of Rs 4,00,000. Dhun Pvt. Ltd. fixes the entry fee to the music concert at Rs 5,000.
You are required to determine the place of supply in respect of the supply(ies) involved in the given scenario.
b.student_answer: In case of supply of event organising services the place of supply shall be the site place where such event is actually performed since the event is performed in Haryana Therefore in the given case the place of supply e & is Haryana in respect of music Concert. And in case of contract between Dhun Put H Ltd and Dhanraj (p) Ltd the place of supply shall be Ludhiana, Punjab, as Dhanraj (p) is registered in Punjab.
b.feedback: The student correctly identified that the place of supply for the event organisation service is Haryana, where the event is held. However, the student incorrectly stated that Dhanraj (P) Ltd. is registered in Punjab (it is registered in Delhi), and did not clearly distinguish between the two supplies: (1) Dhanraj (P) Ltd. to Dhun Pvt. Ltd., and (2) Hotel Dumdum to Dhanraj (P) Ltd. The answer lacks clarity on the place of supply for the booking of the hotel and does not reference the relevant GST provisions.
key_points_covered: Place of supply for event organisation is Haryana (location where event is held)
key_points_missed: Correct registration details of parties
Place of supply for hotel booking (Hotel Dumdum to Dhanraj (P) Ltd.)
Distinction between different supplies involved
Reference to relevant GST provisions (Section 12(7) and 12(3) of IGST Act)

i.question: World Fashions, a registered supplier of designer outfits in Delhi, decides to exhibit its products in a Fashion Show being organised at Hotel Green India, Delhi on 4th January. For the occasion, it gets the service by way of makeover of its models from Glamour Beauty Services Ltd., Mayur Vihar, on 4th January, for which a consideration of ₹ 5,00,000 (excluding GST) has been charged. Glamour Beauty Services Ltd. issued a duly signed tax invoice on 10th February showing the lumpsum amount of ₹ 5,90,000 inclusive of CGST and SGST @ 9% each for the services provided. Examine whether the tax invoice has been issued within the time limit prescribed under law.
i.student_answer:
As per Section 31, - Time Limit to Issue Tax Invoice

The Tax Invoice shall be issued within 30 days from provision of service.

In this case the services are provided on 4th January so tax Invoice should have been issued on 4th February that is within 30 days.

Since in the given case Tax invoice has been issued on 10th February it is not within the time limit prescribed under Section 31 of the CGST Act, 2017
i.feedback: The student correctly identified the relevant section (Section 31), the 30-day time limit for issuing a tax invoice, and applied it accurately to the given dates. The answer clearly concludes that the invoice was not issued within the prescribed time. However, the student could have mentioned that the supplier is not a bank, insurance, or financial institution (for which a 45-day limit applies), and could have referenced the specific rule for services. Minor elaboration on the legal provision would improve completeness.
key_points_covered: Reference to Section 31
30-day time limit for issuing invoice for services
Correct calculation of due date (4th February)
Conclusion that invoice was not issued within time
key_points_missed: Explicit mention that the 30-day limit applies to services other than banking/insurance/financial institutions
Reference to the relevant rule under CGST Rules (Rule 47) for completeness

ii.question: Tax consultant of World Fashions objected to the invoice raised suggesting that the amount of tax charged in respect of the taxable supply should be shown separately in the invoice raised by Glamour Beauty Services Ltd. However, Glamour Beauty Services Ltd. contended that there is no mandatory requirement of showing tax component separately in the invoice. You are required to examine the validity of the objection raised by tax consultant of World Fashions.
ii.student_answer:
The tax charged in respect of taxable supplies should be shown separately as per the provision of the Law.

So the contention of tax consultant of World Fashion & is valid as the amount of tax charged should be shown separately in the tax invoice.
ii.feedback: The student correctly identifies that tax should be shown separately in the invoice and supports the tax consultant's objection. However, the answer lacks reference to the specific legal provision (Section 31 of the CGST Act, 2017 and Rule 46 of CGST Rules), and does not elaborate on the requirement or its rationale. More detail and citation of the relevant law would improve the answer.
key_points_covered: Tax should be shown separately in the invoice
Objection of tax consultant is valid
key_points_missed: Reference to Section 31 of CGST Act and Rule 46 of CGST Rules
Explanation of the legal requirement for separate tax disclosure
Rationale for the requirement

i.question: M/s Balaji Electronics, a registered dealer, is supplying all types of electronic appliances in the State of Karnataka. Its aggregate turnover in the preceding financial year by way of supply of appliances is ₹ 120 lakh. The firm also expects to provide repair and maintenance service of such appliances from the current financial year. With reference to the provisions of the CGST Act, 2017, examine: (i) Whether the firm can opt for the composition scheme, under section 10(1) and 10(2), for the current financial year, as the turnover may include supply of both goods and services?
i.student_answer:
The following persons can opt for composition scheme under section 10(1) & 10(2) whose turnover in the preceeding financial year does not exceed :
a) ₹ 75 Lakh (To Special category state)
b) ₹ 1.5 crore in remaining state.

Since M/s Balaji Electronics is registered in Karnataka, it is eligible for higher threshold limit that is ₹ 1.5 crore since their turnover does not cross the these threshold limit, M/s Balaji Electronics is eligible for composition scheme under section 10(1) & 10(2) for the current financial year.
i.feedback: The student correctly identified the turnover limits for the composition scheme and applied the correct threshold for Karnataka. However, the answer does not address the eligibility in the context of providing both goods and services, nor does it mention the specific service turnover limit (10% of turnover or ₹5 lakh, whichever is higher) as per the CGST Act. The answer also misses mentioning the conditions and restrictions under section 10(2).
key_points_covered: Correct threshold limit for Karnataka
Turnover calculation for eligibility
key_points_missed: Eligibility for supply of both goods and services under composition scheme
Service turnover limit (10% of turnover or ₹5 lakh, whichever is higher)
Conditions and restrictions under section 10(2)

ii.question: If yes, up to what amount, the services can be supplied?
ii.student_answer:
The firm can supply services along with goods other than restaurant & catering services with shall not exceed - Higher of
1) ₹ 500,000
or
2) 10% of the turnover i.e. 1200,000 ₹ (120 x 10%)

M/s Balaji Electronics can supply service upto ₹ 1200,000 and can be in composition levy scheme u/s 10(1) & 10(2).
ii.feedback: The student correctly identified the higher of ₹5,00,000 or 10% of turnover as the service supply limit and applied it to the given turnover, arriving at ₹12,00,000. The answer also references the relevant sections and exceptions. However, there are minor errors in formatting and clarity (e.g., '1200,000' instead of '12,00,000'), and the explanation could be clearer regarding the exclusion of restaurant and catering services.
key_points_covered: Higher of ₹5,00,000 or 10% of turnover as the limit
Correct calculation of 10% of turnover
Reference to composition levy scheme u/s 10(1) & 10(2)
Exclusion of restaurant and catering services
key_points_missed: Clear statement of the turnover base (preceding financial year)
More precise language and clarity in presentation
